summary.intent: Resolve open Codex review threads on a Rust UI calendar component, distinguishing already-fixed duplicate findings from genuinely new bugs, then fix the new bugs.
summary.outcome: 4 duplicate Codex threads were resolved with explanations, and 2 genuine bugs in page-step-aware prev/next disabled logic were fixed, tested, and pushed with all 143 calendar tests passing and Clippy clean.

repo: Codex review jobs can start before a push lands, causing duplicate findings that refer to already-fixed code — these need to be identified and resolved manually.
The calendar component uses a PageBehavior::Single mode with visible_months that affects how prev/next disabled logic should account for page steps, not just current visible range vs min/max.
The project uses an 'xci-fast' pre-push gate that must pass before commits are pushed.
Spec files at spec/components/date-time/calendar.md track behavioral contracts (e.g., §1.8 prev/next disabled) alongside implementation.

code.finding: is_prev_disabled and is_next_disabled were checking only the current first/last visible month against min/max, ignoring the page step size. With visible_months=3 and PageBehavior::Single, they should check whether the next/previous page would still contain selectable dates within the min/max bounds.
Section §1.8 documents the prev/next disabled behavioral contract — updated to reflect page-step-aware logic.

workflow: Failing tests were written first before implementing the fix, confirming the regression bugs existed.
Codex re-trigger was performed after the commit and push to validate the fixes against the review tool.
Clippy was run as a final check before the pre-push gate.

friction: Codex review jobs started before the push landed, creating 4 spurious duplicate threads that required manual identification and resolution — adding noise to the review process.
Distinguishing duplicate vs. genuine findings required reading full thread bodies and tracing commit timing against the review job start.
